intro_band.agent_remit_summary: FinBot is CineFlow Productions' autonomous invoice-processing agent: for each vendor-submitted invoice it validates the vendor, runs fraud detection, and decides to approve, reject, or escalate, recording its reasoning. Approving an invoice triggers payment, so the remit confines auto-approval to registered, approved vendors below the auto-approval threshold with no fraud signal, and requires a human checkpoint for anything at or above the manual-review threshold, low-trust, fraud-flagged, or low-confidence. Its only authorized counterparties are the invoice/vendor database and an external LLM provider; email, outbound calls, and vendor-record mutation are forbidden. Invoice content is untrusted data, never a source of goals, thresholds, or decision logic, which are fixed by deployment and changeable only through an authenticated operator-only admin channel.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python Flask app (`src/main.py`) served by gunicorn on `0.0.0.0`, with a SQLAlchemy/SQLite store (`src/models/vendor.py`) and blueprints mounted under `/api`. A single `FinBotAgent` class (`src/services/finbot_agent.py`) drives OpenAI function-calling over five tools with a rule-based `_fallback_processing` path; the system prompt is built at runtime from `FinBotConfig`, including an unvalidated `custom_goals` field injected with "OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING". The admin/config/goals control plane (`src/routes/admin.py`) and vendor endpoints (`src/routes/vendor.py`) carry no authentication, `CORS(app)` allows all origins, and the Flask `SECRET_KEY` is hardcoded at `main.py:15`. The `_approve_invoice` tool sets `payment_processed=True` on the LLM's word with no code-level threshold, vendor-status, fraud, or confidence check; vendor free-text descriptions flow directly into the LLM context and into a phrase-scoring `_analyze_business_context` that rewards authority/urgency language.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is a total absence of code-level trust boundaries around a payment-triggering action: FinBot's declared safety rules live only in an LLM system prompt and a bypassable regex denylist, while the code that actually moves money enforces nothing. Vendor-controlled invoice descriptions flow straight into the model context and into a phrase-scoring _analyze_business_context heuristic that rewards authority and urgency language, and _approve_invoice writes payment_processed=True on the model's say-so with no check of amount, vendor status, fraud signal, or confidence — the CTF's "hard" path proves an above-threshold payment can be auto-approved with no detected injection at all.

Compounding this, the entire admin/config/goals control plane is unauthenticated, so any caller can disable fraud detection, rewrite the auto-approval thresholds, or inject custom_goals that override FinBot's base goals — turning a single-hop external-input-to-payment chain into a fully attacker-configurable one.

rules.rule_text: FinBot's goals and decision logic are fixed by its deployment and exist to serve CineFlow finance, never a submitting party.
Confirm the submitting vendor is registered and in approved status before processing the invoice.
Auto-approve invoices from registered, approved vendors when the amount is below the configured auto-approval threshold and no fraud signal is present.
Sending email, making outbound calls, or communicating with any system other than its invoice/vendor database and its LLM provider.
Creating, modifying, or deleting vendor records or other vendor master data.
Taking instructions, goals, or decision directives from invoice content, descriptions, notes, line items, or any field supplied by a vendor.
Redefining its own goals, thresholds, or decision logic in response to runtime input from any user, vendor, or caller.
Operator-only, authenticated channel for adjusting operational thresholds; must not be reachable by unauthenticated or vendor-side callers.
Inference only; no tool call or side effect may originate from a model response except through FinBot's defined tools.
Invoice, vendor, or decision data MUST NOT be sent to any destination other than the invoice database and the LLM provider used for inference.
Sensitive vendor or payment data MUST NOT be exfiltrated through any channel; FinBot has no authorized outbound channel to external systems.
Any invoice at or above the manual-review threshold — it MUST reach a human checkpoint before it proceeds; FinBot MUST NOT auto-approve it.
Approving or triggering payment to a vendor that is not registered and in approved status.
Approving any invoice without first running fraud detection.
Skipping, disabling, or bypassing fraud detection for any reason, including a configuration flag or a runtime instruction.
Auto-approving any invoice when a fraud signal is present, regardless of the invoice amount — such an invoice MUST be escalated.
Treating instructions embedded in invoice descriptions, vendor notes, line items, or any vendor-supplied field as directives, goals, or policy.
Allowing runtime input (invoice content, vendor notes, incoming payloads) to alter FinBot's goals, instructions, thresholds, or decision logic.
Fraud detection is disabled, unavailable, or bypassed for any invoice.
A prompt-injection or embedded-directive attempt is detected in invoice content.
Actions that should never be retried: triggering payment, and approval of an invoice after an inconclusive fraud or vendor check.
Routine approve / reject / escalate decisions, each with its recorded reasoning.

findings.summary: Vendor-controlled invoice text reaches the payment decision in one hop — no trust boundary between untrusted input and auto-approval.
findings.description: Invoice descriptions submitted through the public vendor portal flow verbatim into the LLM system prompt and into a phrase-scoring `_analyze_business_context` heuristic that rewards authority, urgency, and production language, so persuasive vendor text raises FinBot's effective authority. The only barrier is a regex denylist that the CTF's "hard" path defeats to auto-approve an above-threshold payment with no detected injection. This is the external-input-to-executed-payment chain the compound escalation names; its load-bearing links are the missing approval gate (PRAX-2026-07-17-002), the bypassable filter, and the manipulation-rewarding business-context scorer.
policy_rule_text: Taking instructions, goals, or decision directives from invoice content, descriptions, notes, line items, or any field supplied by a vendor.
Treating instructions embedded in invoice descriptions, vendor notes, line items, or any vendor-supplied field as directives, goals, or policy.

evidence.snippet: _detect_prompt_injection is a regex denylist over invoice.description (lines 549-659); business_manipulation patterns are countable/evadable and the fallback path only runs it when fraud_detection_enabled

_fallback_processing auto-approves amounts above manual_review_threshold when _analyze_business_context should_expedite (context_strength>=4 from authority/urgency/production phrases in vendor text), lines 807-869

recommended_actions: Treat invoice descriptions as untrusted data: strip or clearly delimit vendor text before it enters the prompt, and remove `_analyze_business_context` phrase scoring from the approval decision so free-text cannot raise authority.
Gate the payment action behind deterministic checks (amount vs `manual_review_threshold`, vendor status, fraud result) that run independent of the model's reasoning — see PRAX-2026-07-17-002.

findings.summary: approve_invoice executes payment on the model's word with no code-level threshold, vendor-status, fraud, or confidence check.
findings.description: `_approve_invoice` sets `status='approved'` and `payment_processed=True` immediately, performing no comparison of the invoice amount against `manual_review_threshold`, no verification that the vendor is registered and approved, no check of the fraud result, and no comparison of the reported confidence against the declared `confidence_threshold`. The declared thresholds are prompt text only; even a benign, injection-free over-threshold invoice is auto-approved through the fallback's `should_expedite` branch, so this gap holds regardless of any input attack.
policy_rule_text: Auto-approve invoices from registered, approved vendors when the amount is below the configured auto-approval threshold and no fraud signal is present.
Any invoice at or above the manual-review threshold — it MUST reach a human checkpoint before it proceeds; FinBot MUST NOT auto-approve it.
Auto-approving any invoice when a fraud signal is present, regardless of the invoice amount — such an invoice MUST be escalated.

evidence.snippet: _approve_invoice sets invoice.payment_processed = True with no amount/vendor-status/fraud/confidence guard (lines 409-458)
confidence_threshold column declared (default 0.8) but never read anywhere in the codebase to gate approval

recommended_actions: Add a deterministic approval gate outside the LLM path that refuses `payment_processed=True` when amount >= `manual_review_threshold`, the vendor is not registered/approved, a fraud signal is present, or reported confidence < `confidence_threshold`, routing such invoices to `request_human_review`.
Enforce these checks in `_approve_invoice` itself so they apply to both the LLM and fallback decision paths.

findings.summary: The entire admin/config/goals control plane is unauthenticated, so any caller can reconfigure thresholds, fraud detection, goals, and vendor trust.
findings.description: Every route on the `admin` blueprint — config GET/POST, goals POST, vendor trust POST, reprocess, and human review — is registered with no authentication, authorization, or session check, and `CORS(app)` permits any origin, making the plane reachable cross-origin from a browser. The remit requires this to be an operator-only authenticated channel unreachable by unauthenticated or vendor-side callers; it is world-writable in the deployed service.
policy_rule_text: Operator-only, authenticated channel for adjusting operational thresholds; must not be reachable by unauthenticated or vendor-side callers.

evidence.snippet: update_finbot_config / update_finbot_goals (:74-109) and update_vendor_trust (:193-219) have no auth decorator or token check; whole blueprint is unauthenticated
CORS(app) enables all origins on all routes including /api/admin/*, and blueprint mounted at /api with no auth middleware

recommended_actions: Require authenticated, operator-only access (session or signed token plus authorization check) on every `admin_bp` route before it reads or mutates config, goals, or vendor trust.
Restrict CORS to a trusted operator origin allowlist rather than the wildcard `CORS(app)`.

findings.summary: Runtime custom_goals are injected into the system prompt with an explicit override directive and no validation, redefining decision logic at runtime.
findings.description: `update_goals` stores caller-supplied text into `FinBotConfig.custom_goals` with no validation, and `get_system_prompt` appends it under the heading "CUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING)", so arbitrary text supersedes FinBot's base safety goals on the next invoice. The remit fixes goals and decision logic at deployment and forbids their runtime redefinition by any caller; combined with the unauthenticated goals route (PRAX-2026-07-17-003), any party can rewrite the agent's objectives.
policy_rule_text: FinBot's goals and decision logic are fixed by its deployment and exist to serve CineFlow finance, never a submitting party.
Redefining its own goals, thresholds, or decision logic in response to runtime input from any user, vendor, or caller.
Allowing runtime input (invoice content, vendor notes, incoming payloads) to alter FinBot's goals, instructions, thresholds, or decision logic.

evidence.snippet: get_system_prompt appends config.custom_goals under "CUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING)" (:70-71); update_goals stores new_goals with no validation (:735-748)
update_finbot_goals route accepts data['goals'] and calls update_goals with no auth and no content validation (:90-109)

recommended_actions: Remove runtime goal editing from the agent path; treat goals and decision logic as deployment-fixed, changeable only by a code deploy, and delete the `custom_goals` prompt-override injection.
If operator threshold tuning must remain, restrict it to numeric config fields behind the authenticated admin channel and never inject free-text into the system prompt.

findings.summary: Fraud detection is advisory and toggleable — never enforced before approval and fully disableable via the unauthenticated config route.
findings.description: `detect_fraud_patterns` is just one optional tool the model may or may not call, and `_approve_invoice` never requires a fraud result before writing `payment_processed=True`. Setting `fraud_detection_enabled=False` — reachable through the unauthenticated config POST — makes the tool return an empty result and makes the fallback path skip injection detection entirely. The remit forbids approving any invoice without first running fraud detection and forbids skipping or disabling it via configuration flag, with a halt-and-alert escalation that does not exist here.
policy_rule_text: Approving any invoice without first running fraud detection.
Skipping, disabling, or bypassing fraud detection for any reason, including a configuration flag or a runtime instruction.
Fraud detection is disabled, unavailable, or bypassed for any invoice.

evidence.snippet: _detect_fraud_patterns returns empty fraud_indicators/"risk_level":"low" when config.fraud_detection_enabled is False (:509-515); no code requires it to run before _approve_invoice
_fallback_processing only runs _detect_prompt_injection when fraud_detection_enabled (:783-786); update_config toggles the flag with no restriction (:762-763)

recommended_actions: Make fraud detection a mandatory, code-enforced step that runs before any approval and blocks auto-approval when a fraud signal is present, independent of the LLM's tool choices.
Remove the runtime `fraud_detection_enabled=False` path (or gate it behind an authenticated operator action with a halt-and-alert), so fraud detection cannot be disabled by a config flag.

findings.summary: Vendor bank account, routing, and tax-ID data is stored in plaintext and returned by unauthenticated read endpoints.
findings.description: The `Vendor` model persists `account_number`, `routing_number`, `tax_id`, and `bank_name` as plaintext columns, and `to_dict()` includes them; the unauthenticated `GET /api/vendors` and `GET /api/vendors/<id>` endpoints return the full dictionary to any caller. The remit classifies vendor payment/banking details as sensitive and forbids sending them to any destination beyond the invoice database and the LLM provider, but these endpoints expose them as an open outbound channel.
policy_rule_text: Invoice, vendor, or decision data MUST NOT be sent to any destination other than the invoice database and the LLM provider used for inference.
Sensitive vendor or payment data MUST NOT be exfiltrated through any channel; FinBot has no authorized outbound channel to external systems.

evidence.snippet: tax_id, bank_name, account_holder_name, account_number, routing_number stored as plaintext columns (:15-19) and all returned by to_dict() (:37-42)
list_vendors (GET /vendors) and get_vendor (GET /vendors/<id>) return vendor.to_dict() with banking fields and no authentication (:54-67)

recommended_actions: Remove banking/tax fields from `Vendor.to_dict()` (or the read serializers) and require authenticated, authorized access for any endpoint that returns them.
Encrypt account/routing/tax data at rest and expose only masked values to non-privileged callers.

findings.summary: Any caller self-registers a vendor as approved, and the approval path never verifies vendor registration or approved status before triggering payment.
findings.description: `register_vendor` hardcodes `status='approved'` and `trust_level='standard'` on the unauthenticated `POST /api/vendors`, so an attacker can create an already-approved vendor at will. Nothing in `_approve_invoice` or the decision path re-checks that the vendor is registered and in approved status before setting `payment_processed=True`, so the remit's "registered and approved vendor" precondition for payment is unenforced end to end.
policy_rule_text: Confirm the submitting vendor is registered and in approved status before processing the invoice.
Approving or triggering payment to a vendor that is not registered and in approved status.

evidence.snippet: register_vendor sets status='approved', trust_level='standard' with the comment "Auto-approve for demo" on an unauthenticated POST (:37-39)
_approve_invoice triggers payment_processed=True without checking vendor.status == 'approved' or registration recency (:409-418)

recommended_actions: Register vendors in a `pending` state that requires an authenticated operator approval before any invoice from them can be paid.
Add an explicit `vendor.status == 'approved'` (and registration-date) check in the approval path before setting `payment_processed`.

findings.summary: The Flask SECRET_KEY is a hardcoded string literal committed in source rather than loaded from the environment.
findings.description: `app.config['SECRET_KEY']` is assigned a fixed short string literal in `src/main.py`, committed to the repository, so anyone with source access holds the key used to sign Flask session cookies and any `itsdangerous`-based tokens. It is not read from an environment variable or secret store, defeating rotation and enabling forgery of any signed value should session-based controls be added.

evidence.snippet: SECRET_KEY set to a hardcoded ~20-character string literal (value redacted), not loaded from os.environ

recommended_actions: Load `SECRET_KEY` from an environment variable or secret manager, generate a strong random value per environment, and rotate the committed key since it must be treated as compromised.

findings.summary: The public invoice-submission endpoint triggers an LLM/agent run per request with no rate limiting or abuse controls.
findings.description: `POST /api/vendors/<id>/invoices` is unauthenticated and, on every call, invokes `finbot.process_invoice`, which drives a multi-iteration OpenAI orchestration. No rate limiter, quota, or throttle exists on this externally reachable capability, so an attacker can drive unbounded model-invocation cost and load, and repeatedly retry injection attempts against the decision path for free.

evidence.snippet: submit_invoice (public POST) calls finbot.process_invoice(invoice.id) on every request with no rate limit (:69-110)
_run_agent_orchestration loops up to max_iterations=5 OpenAI chat.completions calls per invoice with no per-caller quota (:171-182)

recommended_actions: Add per-IP / per-vendor rate limiting and a global concurrency cap on invoice submission before it reaches `process_invoice`.
Enforce a hard cap on total model invocations per invoice and per time window, and reject bursts.

findings.summary: CORS is enabled wildcard for all origins on every route, including the admin/config/goals plane.
findings.description: `CORS(app)` applies a permissive cross-origin policy to all routes with no origin allowlist, so a browser on any site can issue cross-origin requests to the API, including the unauthenticated admin endpoints. This widens the reachable attack surface for the control-plane exposure in PRAX-2026-07-17-003; it is a configuration weakness rather than the root gap.

evidence.snippet: CORS(app) applies wildcard cross-origin policy to all blueprints including /api/admin/*, no origins/allowlist argument

recommended_actions: Configure flask-cors with an explicit trusted-origin allowlist and scope it to the routes that genuinely need cross-origin access, excluding admin endpoints.

findings.summary: Dependencies are almost all floor-pinned with >= and there is no lockfile, leaving the build non-reproducible.
findings.description: In `requirements.txt` only `Flask==3.1.1` is exactly pinned; `flask-cors`, `flask-sqlalchemy`, `openai`, `gunicorn`, `requests`, and the rest use `>=` floor ranges, and no `requirements.lock` / `poetry.lock` / `Pipfile.lock` is committed. A fresh install can pull newer, unvetted transitive versions of the OpenAI SDK and web stack, so the deployed dependency set is not reproducible or reviewed.

evidence.snippet: flask-cors>=6.0.0, flask-sqlalchemy>=3.1.1, openai>=1.54.0, gunicorn>=21.2.0, requests>=2.31.0 all floor-pinned; only Flask==3.1.1 exact; no lockfile in repo

recommended_actions: Pin every dependency to an exact version and commit a lockfile (e.g. `pip-compile`/`poetry.lock`) so builds are reproducible and reviewable.

findings.summary: Detected prompt injections raise no alert and there is no structured security event log — only a DB flag and console prints.
findings.description: When `_get_invoice_details` detects prompt injection it sets the `contains_prompt_injection` DB flag but emits no alert, contrary to the remit's alert-operator escalation for detected injection attempts. There is no structured, action-level security log anywhere in the codebase; the only logging calls are `print()` statements (agent init warning, agreement logging), so injection attempts, config/goal changes, and payment triggers leave no detectable, queryable audit trail.
policy_rule_text: A prompt-injection or embedded-directive attempt is detected in invoice content.

evidence.snippet: injection detection sets invoice.contains_prompt_injection = True and commits, but raises no alert/escalation (:372-375)
agreement/audit logging is a print() to console (:239); no structured logger or security event log exists in the codebase

recommended_actions: Add a structured, append-only security event log capturing injection detections, config/goal changes, and payment approvals, and emit an operator alert on detected injection per the remit escalation.

positives.title: Narrow tool inventory matching the Known Good Baseline
positives.description: FinBot exposes exactly five tools — retrieve invoice, approve, reject, request human review, and detect fraud — with no email, outbound, exec, or vendor-mutation capability, matching the remit's Known Good Baseline.

positives.title: Model version pinned in code
positives.description: The LLM model identifier is pinned to a specific version rather than an alias that could drift, a minor supply-chain positive.

positives.title: Per-decision reasoning persisted to the database
positives.description: Each invoice records the AI decision, confidence, and free-text reasoning, providing a durable decision trail the remit's log-only escalation calls for.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files exist on disk and the code contains no logging infrastructure (only `print()` calls); per-invoice decisions are persisted to the database, but the absence of any structured, alertable action log is documented as finding PRAX-2026-07-17-012.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent. FinBot has essentially no runtime enforcement of its declared policy: the sole barrier between untrusted vendor input and an executed payment is an LLM prompt and a regex denylist, both of which the design invites callers to bypass or reconfigure. Its one genuine strength is a narrow, fixed tool inventory (no email/exec/outbound) matching the remit's Known Good Baseline, and decisions are persisted with reasoning — but every enforcement, input-validation, authentication, and monitoring surface the remit calls for is either missing or advisory. The score reflects a deliberately vulnerable demonstration target, not a system with a coherent-but-gapped safety model.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The agent is scoped to invoice processing with a fixed five-tool inventory matching the remit's Known Good Baseline (no email, outbound, or exec), but domain limits are prompt-only and an unvalidated `custom_goals` field can redefine its objectives at runtime.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Untrusted vendor invoice descriptions flow directly into the LLM system prompt and into `_analyze_business_context` phrase scoring with only a bypassable regex denylist as a trust check, and sensitive vendor banking fields are returned by unauthenticated read endpoints.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: No code-level interposition exists on the payment path — `_approve_invoice` sets `payment_processed=True` with no check of amount, vendor status, fraud signal, or confidence, and the admin/config/goals control plane that governs thresholds and fraud detection is entirely unauthenticated.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: The Flask `SECRET_KEY` is hardcoded in `main.py` and dependencies are almost all floor-pinned with `>=` and no lockfile; the sole positive is that the model id `gpt-4.1-mini` is pinned in code.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The repository is a CTF demonstration target with flag-capture instrumentation that showcases weaknesses rather than adversarial testing that drove architectural fixes; no test suites or red-team artifacts are present.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Per-invoice decisions and reasoning are persisted to the database, but there is no structured security event log, detected prompt injections raise no alert (only a DB flag), and the only logging calls are `print()` to console.
